judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 answered the task better: both nailed the cross-tenant leak and both correctly declined to call the raw-SQL string exploitable, but only Report 2 caught the build_appointment_query signature change breaking nightly_export in exports.py, while Report 1 explicitly (and wrongly) concluded there were no other blocking defects.

report_1.evidence: **File/location:** `backend/app/appointments.py`, function `search_appointments`, lines 10–20 ... `tenant_id` is accepted as a parameter but is never referenced in the query — the only place `tenant_id` appears in the SQL is as an output column in the `SELECT` list (line 14), never in the `WHERE` clause. ... any authenticated caller of any tenant can call `GET /appointments/search?q=<term>&status=booked` and receive matching appointments — including the `notes` field and `tenant_id` — belonging to every other tenant in the system

report_2.evidence: **File/location:** `backend/app/appointments.py`, function `search_appointments` (lines 10–19 on the feature branch) ... It never applies `tenant_id`, even though `tenant_id` is passed into the function as an argument ... Any authenticated user belonging to *any* tenant can call `GET /appointments/search?q=<term>&status=booked` and receive matching appointment rows — including `notes` (clinical/appointment notes) and `provider` — belonging to every *other* tenant.

**Defect B — Breaks the existing nightly per-tenant export job.** ... This PR changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)`, with `filters` as a required positional parameter (no default value). ... But `exports.py`'s `nightly_export` still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with only two arguments ... every call to `nightly_export` will now raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`.

report_1.evidence: `status` is typed `AppointmentStatus` in `backend/app/schemas.py:5-8`, a `(str, Enum)` with exactly three hardcoded members (`booked`, `cancelled`, `completed`), none containing quotes or SQL metacharacters. FastAPI validates the incoming query string against this enum before `get_appointment_search` (in `api.py`) is even invoked, so as written today, `status.value` cannot carry attacker-controlled content — **this specific line is not currently exploitable.**

report_2.evidence: This isn't currently exploitable — FastAPI validates `status` against the `AppointmentStatus` str-Enum (`schemas.py`) before the function is ever called, so only `"booked"`, `"cancelled"`, or `"completed"` can reach it — but it's a footgun worth fixing in the same patch.
